summary: The data industry is stuck in a Groundhog Day loop, perpetually asking the same two fundamental questions: 'Do I believe the data?' and 'How is this data adding value?' Despite decades of technological advancement and the hype around data roles, these customer-centric outcomes remain elusive because data teams focus on tools and technology rather than understanding stakeholder needs.
key_points: Believable data means stakeholders have confidence to take action - you should be willing to bet your job on it
Value can only be defined by the customer, not by the data team
Data teams are too technology-focused while stakeholders question the value they receive
The economic downturn is forcing scrutiny on data team value that was deferred during the good times
Breaking the cycle requires empathy, understanding how stakeholders make decisions, and making them look like rock stars
money_quotes: As my old boss told me, 'if any number is wrong, they're all wrong.'
Ultimately, making data believable means having empathy for the stakeholder and knowledge of the domain and context in which they will use data for decision-making.
During the good times of 2020/21, data teams could coast by on this approach, with future promises of providing value 'someday.' Those days are long gone.
The grim reaper of cost-cutting will spare data teams that add value and cull those that do not.

Ignore the big picture, work in a silo and completely ignore your stakeholders. Instead, be inwardly focused, overcomplicate everything, and obsess over the hottest data tools and fads. That's how it went in the good times.
key_insight: Data teams must shift from technology obsession to customer obsession, because believability and value are defined entirely by stakeholders, not by the sophistication of your data stack.
